pages.full_text:
Thomas is doing all the map reading, very well! We went to Daglan, Nr. Domme to see Monique, Eve's old tennis partner, but she was on holiday in Spain. We met M. Fayolle, her mother, very nice. She suggested that we go to Rocamadour to see the eagles.

      We had lunch in the middle of a small village, Fon[?], next to the stream. Then we drove on to __Rocamadour__. We caught a slanting lift down to see the old shrine. There is a black madonna, enthroned, with a regal child on her lap. Simon thought it a very beautiful statue, slightly off-centre. Hanging from the ceiling is a bell which rings when a miracle is about to happen. Both the statue & the bell are very old & date from before the first of the sackings of Rocamadour in about (13. Warring lords kept sacking Rocamadour because of all its pilgrim riches, including Plantagenet princes: these culminated in the protestants' sacking it & destroying
pages.notes: Page begins mid-sentence — continuation from previous spread. The village name after 'Fon' is partially illegible; could be 'Fontenelle' or similar. The circled '13' near 'sackings of Rocamadour in about (13' likely refers to the 13th century. The superscript 'next day' appears above 'next' in 'next to the stream'. Text cuts off at bottom of page mid-sentence. No handwritten page number visible at top-left. Year cannot be firmly established from this page alone; context (France trip, Dordogne region) is consistent with the volume range 1989–2003.

Page consists entirely of a glued-in photograph of the Black Madonna (Notre-Dame de Rocamadour) — the dark medieval wooden statue of a crowned Madonna with Child on her lap, as described on the left page. Handwritten page number 128 appears in top-right corner. No written caption visible. Some text fragments visible along the left margin edge are the terminal words of lines from the left (L) page bleeding into the overlap zone and have not been re-transcribed here.
